cases.title: RB-47 ELINT Pursuit Case
cases.summary: An RB-47H electronic-intelligence aircraft with six officers was followed for over 700 miles across four states by an object tracked simultaneously by eye, by airborne radar, by ground radar, and by its ELINT receivers as a 2800 MHz emitter. The Condon Report's own staff left the case unexplained; James McDonald's AIAA analysis made it famous.
cases.keyFact: Four independent detection channels — visual, airborne radar, ground radar, and signals intelligence — tracked the same unknown for 700 miles.
cases.official: The Condon study called the episode unidentified/unknown but lacked the correctly dated file during its initial review; Blue Book later carried an airliner identification disputed by the AIAA analysis.
cases.gap: Original radar-scope film is absent and the published reconstruction combines contemporary records with interviews conducted about twelve years later.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the single best multi-sensor case of the pre-digital era, and it sits unexplained inside the very study used to close Blue Book.

cases.title: Magenta 1933 / Gabinetto RS/33 Claim
cases.coordinateBasis: Magenta township, Lombardy — alleged crash vicinity per the RS/33 document set
cases.summary: Documents mailed anonymously to Italian researcher Roberto Pinotti beginning in 1996 describe a craft coming down near Magenta in June 1933, a secret Mussolini-era study group ('Gabinetto RS/33') allegedly chaired by Guglielmo Marconi, wreckage stored at SIAI Marchetti facilities in Vergiate, and a 1945 transfer to U.S. custody. No Italian state-archive corroboration has been located, and Italy's CISU researchers judge the papers likely fabricated. The case went global in 2023 when David Grusch cited Magenta 1933 as the first retrieval.
cases.official: No record of a crash, an RS/33 cabinet, or a Marconi appointment has been found in Italian state archives; CISU assesses the document set as probable fabrication, and no U.S. agency has acknowledged any 1945 transfer.
cases.gap: The anonymous mailer's identity and any archival trace of Gabinetto RS/33 remain unlocated — a single authenticated 1930s document would transform the file. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
sources: MAGENTA-1933 · Pinotti/Lissoni RS/33 document set (anonymous mail, 1996–) / Grusch citation record (2023)

cases.title: Foo Fighters, European Theater
cases.location: Rhine valley air corridor, Western Europe
cases.summary: Night-fighter crews of the 415th NFS reported glowing balls of light pacing their aircraft over the Rhine in 1944–45, nicknamed 'foo fighters' and reported in the wartime press. Allied intelligence suspected German secret weapons; postwar review found the Germans had been seeing them too. No explanation was established.
cases.keyFact: AFHRA’s catalog identifies the 415th Night Fighter Squadron war diary covering 10 February 1943–3 March 1945 as IRIS 60059, call SQ-FI-415-HI, microfilm reel 848, frames 1519–1631. Daily operations reports through 30 November 1944 are IRIS 60074, reel 848, frames 1763–1867; WWII notes are IRIS 1026183, reel 33170. The original scans are not yet secured.
cases.official: Wartime intelligence filed the reports without resolution; postwar explanations (St. Elmo's fire, flak reflections, fatigue) were offered, none demonstrated.
cases.gap: Primary gap: page images from AFHRA IRIS 60059, reel 848, frames 1519–1631, and any underlying December 1944 operations reports needed to compare the public transcriptions against the original record.
cases.whyItMatters: Foo fighters establish that the phenomenon's reporting record predates the 1947 media frame — and that both sides of a world war blamed each other for it.

cases.title: Scandinavian Ghost Rockets
cases.summary: Sweden logged roughly two thousand reports of fast rocket-like objects in 1946, many over lakes; a military dive search at Lake Kölmjärv that July found nothing. Swedish, British, and American intelligence all investigated — Soviet tests were suspected and never confirmed; a substantial fraction of reports resisted the meteor explanation.
cases.keyFact: Sweden’s National Archives publicly identifies the Rymdprojektilkommittén record family as documents concerning space projectiles and the 1946 ‘ghost rockets’ investigation. cases.official: Swedish defense concluded most reports were misidentifications while explicitly leaving a residue unexplained; files have since been released.
cases.gap: Correlation of the Swedish reports with released Soviet-era test records has never been completed. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: Ghost rockets show a modern state running the full investigative playbook — search, intelligence liaison, partial explanation — before the UFO era's vocabulary existed.

cases.summary: Days before the Arnold sighting, Harold Dahl claimed a doughnut-shaped craft dropped slag onto his boat in Puget Sound; Fred Crisman promoted the tale. Two Army Air Forces intelligence officers, Davidson and Brown, died when their B-25 crashed flying samples out — a real fatality attached to what FBI records treat as a hoax Dahl himself disavowed.
cases.keyFact: The first 'physical evidence' story of the modern era was a hoax that still cost two intelligence officers their lives — provenance failure with a body count.
cases.official: FBI and Air Force records classify the claims as a hoax; the B-25 crash was attributed to an engine fire.
cases.gap: Crisman's motives and the slag's origin were never fully documented, feeding decades of secondary conspiracy literature.
cases.whyItMatters: Maury Island predates Arnold and set the pattern: sensational claim, physical 'samples,' institutional response, and a provenance collapse — the field's original cautionary tale.

cases.title: Gorman Dogfight, Fargo
cases.summary: North Dakota ANG pilot George Gorman chased a small white light over Fargo for nearly half an hour, describing intelligent evasive maneuvering; ground witnesses also saw a light. The complete 113-page public Fold3/NARA grouping has now been reconstructed. Its Project Sign records document continued technical investigation, favorable credibility assessments, and internal disagreement before the later Balloon / Astro (Jupiter) evaluation.
cases.keyFact: The complete 113-page public case grouping is recovered — and it shows that the later Balloon/Jupiter shorthand compressed a more contested Project Sign investigation.
cases.official: The later Blue Book index evaluates the case as Balloon / Astro (Jupiter). A retrospective two-target analysis proposes that witnesses first saw a lighted weather balloon and that Gorman later pursued Jupiter or an optical effect; contemporaneous records did not begin with that as an uncontested full-case closure.
cases.gap: The 16 October 1948 transmittal confirms that no tower recording exists because Hector Airport's CAA Control Tower had no recording device. Remaining gaps are the original unsanitized folder, cleaner versions of low-contrast technical sheets, and any formal contemporaneous closure memorandum showing the full Balloon/Jupiter geometry.
cases.whyItMatters: Gorman demonstrates why index labels are not enough: the recovered file records mundane leads, witness credibility, technical testing, internal analytical disputes, and a later compressed resolution in the same paper trail.

cases.title: Mantell Pursuit Fatality
cases.summary: Capt. Thomas Mantell, Kentucky ANG, died when his P-51 crashed during a climb after an object reported over Godman Field in January 1948. The later, well-supported explanation is a then-classified Skyhook balloon and hypoxia — but the death made UFOs a deadly serious Air Force matter overnight.
cases.keyFact: The exact 75-image Project Blue Book case file is now tied to NARA NAID 28930550. It contains contemporary witness statements, flight chronology, crash correspondence and photograph indexes; it also records uncertainty over the oxygen-loss sequence and notes that the reported phenomenon did not readily tally with Venus or a comet.
cases.official: USAF ultimately attributed the object to a Skyhook balloon (classified at the time) and the crash to oxygen starvation above 25,000 feet; early 'Venus' explanations were abandoned.
cases.gap: Primary gap: contemporaneous balloon-launch/Skyhook tracking records and a fully identified crash-investigation/photograph packet that can be cross-walked independently to the Blue Book file.
cases.whyItMatters: Mantell is the cautionary case in both directions: secrecy (Skyhook) manufactured a mystery, and the mystery killed a pilot before the paperwork caught up.
sources: MANTELL-1948 · USAF accident/case record
cases.heroFact: The first fatality in the official record — almost certainly a classified balloon plus hypoxia, and the event that hardened Air Force attitudes for a decade.

cases.whyItMatters: Every 'the government once concluded X' claim descends from this episode; it defines what a credible destroyed-document account looks like when told by an insider.

cases.title: Los Alamos Aerial Phenomena Conference
cases.summary: The Atlas now joins the 25-page AEC conference transcript to the larger 116-page Sandia correspondence file. The combined chain documents organized reporting from December 1948, LaPaz's analysis, particle-collection attempts, the 16 February 1949 conference, Teller's calculations, and continuing concern near sensitive installations.
cases.official: The transcript proves the conference and its classified institutional treatment. It preserves scientific disagreement rather than a settled conclusion: meteor, material-body, electron, psychological, observational, and other possibilities were discussed, while the final page still called the long horizontal paths and absence of noise puzzling.
cases.gap: The cited Air Materiel Command letter, LaPaz's complete standalone report, conference maps, original OSI pilot reports, spectra or photographs, Teller's blackboard work product, Project Grudge follow-up, and a final disposition memorandum remain missing. The Sandia file supplies later copies and summaries, not every originating record.

cases.title: Great Falls / Mariana Film
cases.summary: Nick Mariana filmed two bright discs crossing the sky over Great Falls in 1950 — among the first motion pictures of the phenomenon. The Air Force analyzed the film twice and the Robertson Panel reviewed it; the F-94 reflection explanation conflicts with the documented aircraft timing, and Mariana maintained the clearest frames were not returned.
cases.keyFact: The complete 113-page Blue Book file maps to NARA NAID 28938188 and preserves Mariana’s claim that the first 35 frames were removed and not returned. NARA’s exact motion-picture item is NAID 61935, local identifier 341-PBB-323; its catalog lists a 26-foot 16 mm black-and-white preservation element and reproduction elements, but no camera-original occurrence.
cases.official: USAF attributed the images to sun reflections off two F-94s known to be in the area; the timing reconciliation remains argued.
cases.gap: Primary gap: the reportedly removed first 35 frames, an unambiguous chain-of-custody ledger for every film generation, and first-generation film suitable for modern photogrammetric reanalysis.
cases.whyItMatters: Great Falls ties directly to this atlas's Robertson Panel record: it is half of the physical evidence that panel reviewed before recommending debunking.

cases.title: Tremonton / Newhouse Film
cases.summary: Navy Chief Photographer Delbert Newhouse filmed a dozen maneuvering white objects near Tremonton, Utah. Navy photo analysts spent over a thousand hours on the film and reportedly judged the objects self-luminous and not birds; the Robertson Panel overrode that with a seagull explanation Newhouse rejected, noting he had seen the objects closely before filming.
cases.keyFact: The exact 97-image Blue Book file is NARA NAID 28943418 and the motion-picture item is NAID 61937. NARA lists 42-foot 16 mm reference and preservation elements, while the digital transfer slate identifies its source as ‘16mm RSK (P Copy).’ The Navy analysis records that brittle film was duplicated and warns that the analysis film was ‘a duplicate of a copy.’
cases.official: Robertson Panel: seagulls in sunlight. Navy Photographic Interpretation Center's contrary internal assessment survives only in summaries.
cases.gap: Primary gap: the first-generation 16 mm camera film and complete laboratory chain-of-custody/measurement worksheets needed to determine which analysis used which film generation.
cases.whyItMatters: Tremonton is the other half of the Robertson Panel's evidence base, and its analyst-versus-panel split prefigures every modern sensor-interpretation fight.
sources: TREMONTON-1952 · Newhouse film / Robertson Panel record
cases.heroFact: The Navy's own photographic laboratory and the CIA's panel reached opposite conclusions about the same frames — the clearest documented analyst split of the era.

cases.title: Special File: T. Townsend Brown Electrogravitics
cases.summary: Thomas Townsend Brown spent three decades promoting charged-capacitor thrust (the 'Biefeld–Brown effect'), pitched the Pentagon a disc-shaped electrogravitic craft program in 1952 (Project Winterhaven), and demonstrated flying test rigs at Bahnson Laboratories in 1957–58, captured on film. Mainstream analysis attributes the thrust to ionic wind, not gravity control.
cases.keyFact: The documented root of 'antigravity' lore: real experiments, a real Pentagon proposal, filmed demonstrations — and a conventional electrohydrodynamic explanation.
cases.official: No service adopted Winterhaven; published physics attributes Biefeld–Brown thrust to ion momentum transfer in air, which vanishes in vacuum tests.
cases.gap: The distribution list and any classified service evaluations of Project Winterhaven — and the 1956 'Electrogravitics Systems' industry survey's sponsorship — remain incompletely documented.
cases.whyItMatters: Brown's file separates a genuinely documented research thread from the propulsion mythology built on top of it — the atlas's cleanest example of real paper feeding unreal claims.

cases.title: Robertson Panel Recommendations
cases.summary: The CIA-convened Robertson Panel recommended a public education and debunking program using mass media, psychologists, training films and organizations including Walt Disney, while retaining a modest technical capability for residual cases and watching civilian UFO groups for possible influence or subversion.
cases.official: The panel's formal posture treated UFO reporting chiefly as an air-defense communications and public-perception problem. It recommended training and debunking, but also said a modest ATIC section should continue following cases of possible scientific-intelligence value.
cases.gap: The recommendation proves what the panel proposed, not how fully it was implemented. Still needed are tasking records, training products, media contacts, civilian-group monitoring reports and the two access-restricted records noted in the released file.
sources: CIA-UAP-002 · Robertson Panel report, 42-page declassified CIA file
cases.coordinateBasis: Washington, DC
cases.keyFact: The CIA did not merely study public UFO interest; the panel recommended an organized debunking and monitoring strategy using mass media, psychologists, and civilian-group surveillance.
cases.whyItMatters: It documents narrative-management recommendations in primary-source form. That weakens any claim that public ridicule was purely organic.
cases.keyQuote: This education could be accomplished by mass media such as television, motion pictures, and popular articles.

cases.title: Levelland Vehicle-Interference Wave
cases.summary: Over a few hours on the night of November 2–3, 1957, multiple independent drivers around Levelland reported a glowing egg-shaped object accompanied by stalled engines and dead headlights that recovered after it departed. The sheriff saw a distant light; Blue Book sent one investigator and attributed the wave to 'ball lightning' despite no documented storm.
cases.keyFact: The benchmark electromagnetic-effects case: separate, mutually unaware witnesses reporting the same engine-and-lights failure signature within hours.
cases.official: Project Blue Book: ball lightning / severe electrical storm. Weather records for the area that night document no storm, a discrepancy critics have never resolved.
cases.gap: No systematic vehicle inspection or electrical analysis was performed on any affected car — the physical evidence was driven home.
cases.whyItMatters: Levelland defined the vehicle-interference category that recurs for decades; its one-investigator handling is also a case study in institutional under-response.

cases.summary: The January 1957 Conference on the Role of Gravitation in Physics (now known as GR1), organized by Bryce and Cécile DeWitt at UNC Chapel Hill, was funded through aviation money — Agnew Bahnson's Institute of Field Physics — with proceedings issued as a WADC technical document. A legitimate physics milestone (gravitational waves, quantum gravity) later woven into 'antigravity program' lore.
cases.keyFact: A genuine landmark physics conference, bankrolled by aviation-industry gravity-control enthusiasm and archived by the Air Force — the factual kernel inside every 1950s antigravity-program narrative.
cases.official: The conference produced foundational general-relativity research; no craft program followed, and its Air Force connection was archival, not operational.
cases.gap: Correspondence between Bahnson's institute, the DeWitts, and interested aircraft firms is only partially collected in public archives.
cases.whyItMatters: Chapel Hill anchors the 1950s 'gravity control' era in verifiable history, letting the atlas separate what the aerospace industry actually funded from what the lore claims it built.

cases.title: Condon Committee Pre-Judgment Record
cases.summary: Before the Colorado study was complete, project administrator Robert Low wrote that it should appear objective to the public while presenting scientists with a group of non-believers having an almost zero expectation of finding a saucer. The later Condon Report was used by the Air Force to support ending Project Blue Book.
cases.official: The Air Force relied on the Condon Report's recommendation against further extensive UFO study when it terminated Project Blue Book in 1969. That policy decision does not by itself resolve whether the pre-study language shaped case selection, analysis or the final synthesis.
cases.gap: The local source package lacks a facsimile of the Low memorandum. Also missing from this dossier are the complete internal methodology and case-selection correspondence, responses to the memorandum, and the Air Force decision trail comparing the report's synthesis with unresolved case chapters.

cases.title: Malmstrom AFB Missile Shutdown Incident
cases.summary: A contemporaneous 341st Strategic Missile Wing history records that all ten Echo Flight launch facilities lost strategic alert nearly simultaneously on 16 March 1967. The surviving official engineering record did not establish a UFO cause; later witness accounts and UFO rumors remain separate testimony.
cases.official: The unit history confirms the coordinated alert failure and a large technical investigation. An August 1967 engineering summary said the only UFO association known to its author was rumor and reported that no unusual activity or sightings were documented in the investigation material then reviewed.
cases.gap: Missing or incomplete in the public package are original maintenance logs, alarm printouts, fault-isolation worksheets, security-police blotters, full witness interviews, communications traffic, and a complete custody chain for every reproduced page. The downloaded full package is a private mirror of Air Force FOIA material, not an Air Force-hosted original.

cases.title: Coyne Helicopter Near-Collision
cases.summary: Four U.S. Army Reserve helicopter crew members reported a lighted object closing on their UH-1H near Mansfield, radio interruption, a green beam, and an unexpected climb while descent controls were reportedly set. A 1979 CUFOS monograph preserves interviews, ground-witness accounts, and reproductions of Army hazard paperwork.
cases.keyFact: The crew's report is documented and named, but the claimed uncommanded climb rests on testimony and cockpit instruments rather than preserved flight-recorder data.
cases.official: The encounter was placed on record through Army Operational Hazard reporting, but no official Army identification or analytical conclusion was located. The strongest published reconstruction is an independent CUFOS investigation, not a military finding.
cases.gap: The public package lacks the original Army case file, contemporaneous ATC recordings, complete aircraft maintenance and instrument-inspection records, raw interview tapes, radar data, and an independent flight-dynamics reconstruction from original telemetry.
cases.whyItMatters: Coyne combines disciplined military witnesses with real paperwork while retaining the central evidentiary weakness: no raw telemetry or complete maintenance record survives in the public package.

cases.title: Pascagoula River Abduction Report
cases.summary: Charles Hickson and Calvin Parker reported being taken aboard a craft by robotic entities while fishing on the Pascagoula River. Left alone in a sheriff's interview room with a hidden recorder, the two continued discussing the event in evident distress — the case's unique covert-credibility artifact. Parker stayed largely silent about it until 2018.
cases.keyFact: The only abduction narrative tested by a covert recording: deputies secretly taped the witnesses alone, and the tape captured continued private distress, not rehearsal.
cases.official: No agency investigated; Project Blue Book had closed in 1969. Skeptical readings propose a hypnagogic episode; the secret tape remains the disputed centerpiece.
cases.gap: The full original sheriff's tape and complete interview logs have never been archived publicly in one authenticated set. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: Pascagoula supplies what every testimony case lacks — a moment when the witnesses did not know they were being evaluated.
sources: PASCAGOULA-1973 · sheriff's covert recording / witness record trail

cases.title: Special File: Billy Meier Contact Corpus
cases.summary: Eduard 'Billy' Meier has claimed ongoing contact with 'Plejaren' beings since 1975, producing hundreds of daylight photographs, films, sound recordings, and metal samples through his FIGU organization. Investigations repeatedly reproduced his imagery with small models — the 'wedding cake' craft resembles household objects — and most researchers, including sympathetic ones, judge the corpus fabricated. The following remains global and active.
cases.keyFact: The largest single-source evidence corpus in UFOlogy — and the most reproduced-by-model: volume of material is not weight of evidence.
cases.official: No agency has investigated; independent analyses attribute the photographs to miniature models, and early positive metallurgy claims were never replicated under custody.
cases.gap: Meier's original negatives and the disputed metal samples have never been examined under neutral, documented custody — the standing offer that would settle the file.
cases.whyItMatters: Meier is the atlas's volume-versus-provenance lesson: five decades of prolific 'evidence' that fails the same chain-of-custody test a single satellite photo must pass.

cases.title: Tehran F-4 Intercept Case
cases.summary: A declassified U.S. defense message records two Iranian F-4 intercept attempts, a reported radar lock at 27 nautical miles, communications and instrumentation failures near the object, and a weapons-control failure during a missile attempt. The message preserves the military report but not the raw operational data needed to reproduce it.
cases.official: The released USDAO/JCS message records the Iranian military account, radar-lock report and equipment effects, then states that more information would be forwarded when available. It gives no final object identification and includes no reproduced radar, maintenance or radiation-analysis results.
cases.gap: Still absent are raw radar plots or tapes, ground-controller and communications logs, F-4 maintenance and fault records, missile-system diagnostics, named crew statements, and the promised follow-up or radiation-test results.

cases.title: Cash-Landrum Injury Case
cases.summary: Betty Cash, Vickie Landrum, and her grandson Colby reported a diamond-shaped object venting flame over the road, escorted by what they counted as more than twenty CH-47-type helicopters. Their subsequent hospital-documented symptoms resembled radiation exposure. Their federal lawsuit was dismissed in 1986 after every service denied owning such a craft.
cases.keyFact: The injuries are on hospital paper and the lawsuit is on court paper; only the object has no paper — the U.S. government's defense was that the craft could not be theirs.
cases.official: All military branches denied operating the object or helicopters; the court dismissed for lack of a provable government instrumentality.
cases.gap: Complete medical baselines predating the event and any Army helicopter flight logs for that night have never been reconciled publicly. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: Cash-Landrum is the only case where U.S. courts formally adjudicated UAP-attributed injuries — and resolved it by proving an absence of ownership, not an absence of event.

cases.title: Rendlesham Forest / RAF Bentwaters Incident
cases.summary: Lieutenant Colonel Charles Halt's 13 January 1981 memorandum documents reports of unexplained lights near RAF Woodbridge and a later observation in which Halt participated. The UK Ministry of Defence concluded the incident was not of defence significance and did not conduct a further investigation.
cases.official: The National Archives says Halt reported lights near the rear gate, servicemen investigated on two nights, and a parliamentary-response note found nothing of defence interest. It also states that a single-sheet report is the only record of the event itself in its custody, while related files are mainly later enquiries.
cases.gap: The archive lacks a contemporaneous full investigative file, complete security logs, original witness statements from every participant, validated radar data, complete photographic evidence, and a fully resolved timeline. Halt's memo is a report, not proof of the object's identity or performance.

cases.title: Special File: Bennewitz–Doty Disinformation Affair
cases.summary: Albuquerque businessman Paul Bennewitz, monitoring signals near Kirtland AFB, was fed fabricated UFO material in an AFOSI counterintelligence operation in which agent Richard Doty later admitted participating on camera. Bennewitz's deteriorating mental health ended in hospitalization; several enduring UFOlogy motifs (underground bases, treaty lore) trace to the planted material.
cases.keyFact: A documented case of the U.S. government manufacturing UFO mythology on purpose — with the operator's on-camera admission.
cases.official: AFOSI has never formally acknowledged the operation; Doty's admissions and released Kirtland-era documents constitute the record.
cases.gap: The operation's authorizing paperwork and full scope (who else received planted material) remain unreleased.
cases.whyItMatters: Bennewitz–Doty is the atlas's contamination-control file: it proves some lore is state-authored noise, which every other testimony case must be filtered against.

cases.title: Trans-en-Provence Trace Case
cases.summary: After Renato Nicolaï reported a saucer-shaped object landing briefly on his property, France's state UAP office GEPAN (within CNES) collected soil and plant samples within days. Its Technical Note 16 documented mechanical compaction, heating of the soil, and chlorophyll degradation in nearby plants — the most rigorous government trace analysis on record.
cases.keyFact: A state space-agency laboratory analyzed the landing site and published anomalous soil and plant findings under its own name.
cases.official: GEPAN concluded an unexplained physical event occurred; later skeptical re-readings propose vehicle tires and cement dust, which GEPAN-era scientists dispute.
cases.gap: The complete raw laboratory data behind Technical Note 16 has never been fully released for independent reanalysis.
cases.whyItMatters: Trans-en-Provence is the world benchmark for trace-case methodology — what every landing report should have been subjected to, done exactly once.
sources: GEPAN-1981 · Note Technique No 16, 'Enquête 81/01 — Analyse d'une Trace' (CNES/GEIPAN official PDF)

cases.summary: In December 1984, producer Jaime Shandera received undeveloped film of an alleged 1952 'Eisenhower Briefing Document' naming a twelve-member group overseeing a 1947 disc recovery. AFOSI declared the documents fabricated and the FBI's public file carries a handwritten 'BOGUS' across the pages; researchers documented format and dating anachronisms.
cases.keyFact: The canonical provenance-failure case: documents that arrived with no chain of custody, and whose own investigative file is annotated with one word — BOGUS.
cases.official: AFOSI: the documents are 'completely bogus'; the FBI closed its inquiry on that basis. Proponents argue later 'MJ-12' drops corroborate the first; document examiners largely disagree.
cases.gap: The identity of the original mailer has never been established, which is itself the case's defining evidentiary hole.
cases.whyItMatters: MJ-12 is the atlas's control case: extraordinary content evaluated purely on chain of custody, and failing — the standard every leaked document must be measured against.

cases.title: Special File: Bob Lazar / S-4
cases.summary: In November 1989 Bob Lazar told KLAS-TV's George Knapp he had reverse-engineered saucer propulsion at a site called S-4 near Groom Lake, powered by 'element 115.' His employment trail is thin (a W-2 stub and a Los Alamos phone-directory listing), his claimed MIT/Caltech degrees are unverified, and element 115 as later synthesized (moscovium) has no stable isotope matching his claims.
cases.keyFact: The most influential whistleblower narrative in UFOlogy rests on a nearly empty verifiable record: one pay stub, one phone-book line, and no confirmable education history.
cases.official: The USAF and DOE do not acknowledge any S-4 facility; Los Alamos records place Lazar at most as a contractor-period technician.
cases.gap: Any payroll, clearance, or badge record placing Lazar inside EG&G special projects would settle the case and has never surfaced.
cases.whyItMatters: Lazar defined the modern Area 51 mythos and the template for every 'insider' narrative since; the file documents exactly where testimony outruns paper.

cases.summary: About sixty children at Ariel School reported a landed craft and small beings at the edge of the schoolyard during morning break. They were interviewed on camera within days, including by BBC correspondent Tim Leach and later Harvard psychiatrist John Mack; adult follow-up interviews decades later remain broadly consistent.
cases.keyFact: Sixty child witnesses, filmed within days, telling structurally consistent accounts that have held up in adult re-interviews — the strongest mass-witness testimony corpus in the record.
cases.official: No Zimbabwean government investigation was conducted; skeptical readings attribute the event to suggestion cascading through the group.
cases.gap: The complete unedited 1994 interview footage has never been archived in one public collection.
cases.whyItMatters: Ariel is the benchmark for testimony-only evidence: no instruments, no traces, but a witness corpus large and consistent enough that explanation attempts must engage it seriously.

cases.title: Special File: 'Alien Interview' / Victor Tape
cases.summary: 'Area 51: The Alien Interview' (1997), produced and distributed by Rocket Pictures, presented footage said to show an extraterrestrial being questioned at the S-4 facility. It was supplied by a disguised whistleblower using the name 'Victor,' whose identity — and the tape's chain of custody — were never verified. Effects artists and analysts remain split, with puppetry the prevailing skeptical reading; 'Victor' resurfaced once in a 2008 follow-up interview.
cases.keyFact: Footage whose entire evidentiary weight rests on an anonymous source who withheld provenance by design — content that cannot be promoted to evidence no matter how it looks.
cases.official: No agency has acknowledged the footage; the production company marketed it as unverified, leaving authenticity claims entirely to 'Victor.'
cases.gap: Victor's identity, the original tape, and any transfer records between S-4 (claimed) and Rocket Pictures have never surfaced. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: Paired with MJ-12, the Victor tape completes the atlas's provenance lesson for the video era: anonymous chain-of-custody claims fail identically whether the artifact is paper or tape.
sources: VICTOR-1997 · 'Area 51: The Alien Interview' release record / 2008 follow-up interview

cases.title: Special File: COMETA Report
cases.summary: 'UFOs and Defense: What Should We Prepare For?' — a 1999 report by a private committee of French generals, admirals, and senior engineers associated with the IHEDN defense institute — reviewed the best-documented cases and judged the extraterrestrial hypothesis the most plausible remaining candidate, urging defense preparedness. It was delivered to France's President and Prime Minister.
cases.keyFact: Senior defense establishment figures of a NATO-allied state put their names on a report calling the ET hypothesis the leading unexcluded explanation.
cases.official: The French government accepted delivery without endorsement; GEIPAN continues as the state's operational UAP office.
cases.gap: The full English text is now held in the atlas corpus (COMETA-1999 file set); the remaining gap is the committee's underlying case dossiers, which were never published.
cases.whyItMatters: COMETA is the strongest institutional-credibility artifact in the field: not a leak, not anonymous — signed, delivered, and never retracted.
sources: COMETA-1999 · report text (English translation, archive.org mirror) / IHEDN-associated authorship record

cases.title: O'Hare Airport Disc Sighting
cases.summary: United Airlines ramp and flight-deck personnel reported a metallic disc hovering over gate C17 before it shot upward, reportedly punching a hole through the overcast. The FAA initially denied any report existed, acknowledged it after a FOIA request, attributed it to weather, and declined to investigate.
cases.keyFact: Trained aviation professionals reported a disc over a major U.S. airport and the FAA's documented response was to log it as weather and not investigate.
cases.official: FAA: a 'weather phenomenon,' with no safety investigation warranted; United declined to discuss the reports.
cases.gap: Tower tapes and the full set of employee statements were never released; no radar analysis was published.
cases.whyItMatters: A modern daylight case at controlled airspace showing the institutional reporting gap AARO-era reforms were later built to close.
sources: OHARE-2006 · NARCAP TR-10 Case 18 incl. FAA tower-tape transcript / FAA FY2007 FOIA logs (GovernmentAttic) / FAA FOIA acknowledgment trail

cases.title: Stephenville Radar-Correlated Sightings
cases.summary: Multiple witnesses reported unusual lights around Dublin and Stephenville. A MUFON study analyzed 2.8 million FAA radar returns and identified non-transponder tracks near some witness directions, while explicitly acknowledging timing, association, and radar-precision limits.
cases.keyFact: The radar data are governmental; the correlation and extraordinary-performance claims are an independent interpretation that must be tested against the missing raw dataset and timing uncertainties.
cases.official: The FAA released radar data; it did not issue a finding that the returns were a UAP. The Air Force later acknowledged ten F-16s operating in the area after an initial denial. The unknown-track interpretation belongs to the independent Schulze-Powell analysis.
cases.gap: The local package lacks the original 2.8-million-return dataset and reproducible analysis code, military radar data, complete airborne radar recordings, unredacted squadron logs, and enough synchronized witness timing to prove that each visual report corresponds to a particular return.
cases.whyItMatters: Stephenville shows both the power and limit of FOIA: official sensor data can challenge an initial denial without automatically proving that a specific return was the reported object.

cases.title: Special File: AAWSAP / AATIP & Skinwalker
cases.summary: The DIA's Advanced Aerospace Weapon System Applications Program (AAWSAP, ~$22M, 2008–2012) contracted Robert Bigelow's BAASS, whose portfolio included studies at Skinwalker Ranch. The AATIP effort continued informally under Luis Elizondo until his 2017 resignation — the chain that produced the New York Times story and public release context for the Navy videos already in this atlas.
cases.keyFact: The funding paper trail is real and public: a DIA program, a Senate patron, a contractor, and a ranch — the documented plumbing behind the 2017 disclosure era.
cases.official: DoD confirmed AATIP existed while disputing its scope and Elizondo's role; AAWSAP contract documents and DIRD technical papers have been released via FOIA.
cases.gap: The full set of BAASS field reports from the ranch and the complete AAWSAP deliverables list remain unreleased. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: It connects this atlas's modern sensor cases to their institutional origin: Nimitz-era footage reached the public through this exact program lineage.

cases.title: Aguadilla Puerto Rico Object
cases.summary: A U.S. Customs and Border Protection aircraft recorded infrared imagery near Rafael Hernández Airport. AARO’s 2025 reconstruction concluded that two slow-moving objects remained over land; the earlier SCU model inferred high-speed, transmedium motion from a different trajectory solution.
cases.official: AARO assesses with high confidence that the objects did not exhibit anomalous behavior or transmedium capabilities and with moderate confidence that they were sky lanterns. AARO modeled approximately 3.6 m/s motion over land, consistent with the reported wind direction and speed.
cases.gap: The public package does not include AARO’s complete reproducible STK scene, raw platform telemetry, calibrated sensor model, synchronized airport/ATC records, or all wind inputs. Those records are needed to test the official and SCU trajectories from identical inputs.
sources: DOD-110692805-AGUADILLA · Official Puerto Rico objects video
AARO-PUERTO-RICO-2025 · AARO Puerto Rico case resolution

cases.coordinateBasis: Aguadilla/Rafael Hernández Airport public coordinate
cases.keyFact: AARO and SCU do not merely interpret the same track differently; they reconstruct materially different tracks from the same line-of-sight imagery.
cases.whyItMatters: Aguadilla demonstrates how a transmedium claim can rise or fall on modeled geometry, contrast loss, and assumptions about whether the target remained over land.

cases.title: Chilean Navy IR Video (CEFAA)
cases.summary: A Chilean Navy helicopter's WESCAM IR camera recorded an object trailing plumes over the sea in 2014. CEFAA, Chile's official UAP office, released the video in 2017 after a two-year study concluded it was unexplained. Independent analysts subsequently matched the object to a scheduled airliner's contrails — a correction CEFAA absorbed publicly.
cases.keyFact: A government office studied a video for two years and called it unexplained; open-source analysts identified it in days — the field's best documented lesson in method over authority.
cases.official: CEFAA released the case as unexplained in 2017; the flight-track identification (IB6830 candidate) is now the prevailing reading.
cases.gap: CEFAA never published a formal retraction document, leaving the file technically open in Chile's records. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: This is the atlas's calibration case for official credulity: institutional provenance guarantees the footage, not the conclusion.

cases.title: Pantex Unidentified Object Report
cases.summary: A seven-page Pantex security packet documents ground-surveillance-radar detection, protective-force observations, CROWS-camera tracking, tower video, and Sandia-enhanced images of an unidentified airborne object. The packet conflicts internally on whether the incident occurred on 1 or 2 September 2015 and supplies no final identification.
cases.official: Pantex treated the object under its approved UAS response plan, secured sensitive-area gates, followed it offsite, sent video to Sandia National Laboratories, and transferred incident evidence to an FBI agent. The report says the object was not threatening and did not approach sensitive assets.
cases.gap: Still missing are native radar-track data, original tower and CROWS video, original image files, Sandia's analytical product, complete witness statements, the FBI receipt and investigative file, and a final attribution or closure memorandum. The released pages reproduce imagery but do not provide enough resolution or analysis to identify the object.

cases.title: GoFast Navy Sensor Event
cases.summary: A U.S. Navy infrared clip created the impression of a small object moving rapidly above the ocean. NASA and AARO analyses concluded that platform motion and parallax can explain the apparent speed without extraordinary performance.
cases.official: AARO assesses with high confidence that GoFast did not exhibit anomalous behavior. Its reconstruction places the object near 13,000 feet and models a wind-speed-compatible range of motion; NASA independently estimated about 40 mph while explicitly noting neglected wind effects and resulting uncertainty.
cases.gap: AARO’s published result is model-based. The public record does not include the original uncompressed sensor file, complete aircraft track, full vertical wind profile, range-quality provenance, or reproducible model package needed to independently rerun every assumption.

cases.gap: Sonar records that would confirm or refute the water entry have never been released. Evidence-depth audit completed; context images/manifests are not treated as primary corroboration.
cases.whyItMatters: Omaha extends this atlas's Nimitz thread into the swarm era and frames the modern problem: perfect chain of custody, persistent ambiguity.

cases.title: Langley AFB Drone Incursions
cases.summary: The Defense Department confirms that unauthorized unmanned aerial systems repeatedly appeared over and around Joint Base Langley-Eustis in December 2023. The public record does not identify the operators or release the sensor data needed to characterize every object.
cases.keyFact: DOD publicly confirms repeated unauthorized UAS incursions; it has not publicly attributed the operators.
cases.official: A 15 October 2024 Pentagon briefing called the events incursions of unauthorized UAS and said they did not appear to exhibit hostile intent. A 30 April 2025 DOD account described the systems as being of unknown origin and acknowledged that the department then had limited ability to detect, track, characterize, disrupt, or defeat them.
cases.gap: Still missing are the complete incursion chronology, base and FAA sensor logs, radar tracks, imagery, electronic-signature data, response logs, airspace restrictions, recovered material if any, and an attribution finding. Unknown origin or operator does not by itself establish exotic technology.
cases.whyItMatters: A serious counter-UAS failure with unresolved attribution—not automatically exotic UAP.

cases.title: Northeastern Orb Surveillance
cases.summary: Seven released FBI records document witness-video collection, field interviews, agent surveillance, direct agent observations and a follow-up site survey. Agents described lights moving erratically and took five mostly blurry photographs; a later daylight survey found nothing unusual at ground or treetop level.
cases.official: The released FBI records establish that agents investigated and personally observed reported light activity. They do not publish an analytical identification or closure finding; the follow-up survey records no unusual ground or treetop feature and notes that the agents' five photographs were mostly blurry and provided little context.
cases.gap: The public packet withholds the exact site, witness and agent identities, dates and several operational details. Also absent are the referenced original witness videos, the five full-resolution agent photographs with per-file mapping and metadata, any laboratory or imagery analysis, chain-of-custody records and a disposition or closure memorandum.

cases.summary: An airborne radar operator detected an unidentified object via raw radar skinpaint (not transponder) over 3.5 hours of intermittent contact off the Northern California coast. The investigator flagged this as the first confirmed radar skinpaint of a UAP — 'significant' and 'unprecedented.'

cases.summary: A flaming greenish-blue object was observed at 2,500 feet with an estimated speed of 1,500 mph over the U.S. Naval Operating Base at Kodiak, Alaska. The observation lasted 15 seconds and was reported by Lt. Herring. The case appears in the Army Counter Intelligence Corps Flying Discs file (342-HS1, 1949).
cases.official: No conventional explanation located in the available CIC file pages. The estimated speed of 1,500 mph in 1949 — three years before the D.C. flap — far exceeded any operational aircraft.
cases.gap: Full CIC investigation file is 143 pages; follow-up reports and any official conclusion may exist in the remaining pages. The Kodiak case appears on pp. 51-52.
sources: DOW-UAP-D084 · Army CIC Flying Discs 1949 (342-HS1, 143pp), pp. 51-52 — Report by Lt. Herring, Kodiak Alaska
cases.keyFact: 1,500 mph in 1949 — three years before the D.C. radar flap and seven years before the U-2 — observed by a naval officer over a U.S. military base in Alaska.
cases.whyItMatters: Fills an Alaska geographic gap. The greenish-blue color connects to the green fireball / Los Alamos pattern already in the Atlas. Military base overflight with a named officer witness. The speed estimate predates virtually all known high-speed aircraft programs.
cases.keyQuote: Flaming greenish-blue object at 2,500 feet, estimated speed 1,500 mph.
cases.quoteSource: DOW-UAP-D084, Army CIC Flying Discs 1949 (342-HS1)
cases.heroFact: A naval officer reported a flaming greenish-blue object at 1,500 mph over Kodiak Naval Base in 1949 — three years before the Washington D.C. flap.
cases.significance: High significance — early speed data, military base

cases.title: R5D Pilot — Bright Disc That Circled the Aircraft
cases.location: Washington D.C. area (R5D night training flight)
cases.coordinateBasis: Washington D.C. area, approximate location of R5D training flight
cases.summary: An R5D aircraft on a routine night training flight at 4,500 feet encountered a brilliant white light that traversed the windshield in two seconds, climbed at terrific speed, then turned sharply across the aircraft's path. The pilot described a 'very bright disc, brighter than white phosphorus, with two definite blinking red lights' on apparent wingtips — but no wings were visible. The object circled the aircraft before departing at high speed on bearing 230 degrees.
cases.official: Case appears in the D.C. 1952 Blue Book case files. No official conclusion located in the available pages.
cases.gap: The specific Blue Book case number, investigator identity, official conclusion, and any follow-up reports have not been located.

cases.keyFact: A pilot reported a 'bright disc, brighter than white phosphorus' with red lights on 'wingtips' but no wings visible — the object circled the aircraft before departing at high speed.
cases.whyItMatters: One of the most dramatic pilot-UFO encounters in the declassified Blue Book record. The circling behavior and 'no wings' observation make this qualitatively different from a balloon, meteor, or astronomical misidentification. Trained pilot observer at low altitude.

cases.title: Gemini 7 Astronaut 'Bogey' Report
cases.coordinateBasis: Low Earth orbit, Gemini 7 mission
cases.summary: Gemini 7 Commander Frank Borman reported a 'bogey at ten o'clock high' during the mission. Houston asked if it was the booster or a 'natural sighting.' Borman and Lovell observed hundreds of particles, the booster, and a third unidentified object. NASA PAO later confirmed 'the reference to the third and unidentified object... was a bogey' — distinct from both the booster and the particles.
cases.official: NASA PAO confirmed the third object was an unidentified 'bogey.' No further official explanation located.
cases.gap: The Gemini 7 mission transcript (NASA Tape T-00763(Rlb)) is the primary record. No follow-up analysis, radar correlation, or official identification of the bogey has been located.
sources: NASA-T-00763 · Gemini 7 Technical Crew Debriefing / Mission Transcript (December 1965)
cases.keyFact: A NASA astronaut reported an unidentified 'bogey' during a Gemini mission — the PAO confirmed it was a third object distinct from the booster and particles.
cases.whyItMatters: Astronaut-reported UAP during an operational NASA mission. The PAO confirmation that it was 'unidentified' (not the booster, not particles) distinguishes this from the routine debris observations common in orbital missions.
cases.keyQuote: The reference to the third and unidentified object... was a bogey.
cases.quoteSource: NASA PAO, Gemini 7 mission transcript (T-00763)

The speed claim is extraordinary: no operational aircraft of any nation in 1955 could approach Mach 10. The report adds Soviet/USSR coverage to the Atlas and connects to the broader pattern of high-speed UAP observations across multiple nations and decades.

cases.title: Special File: CIA OSE Study Group — 1952 Internal UFO Review
cases.summary: In August 1952, following the Washington D.C. radar flap and a formal query from the White House, CIA's Office of Scientific Intelligence (OSI) convened an internal study group to review the UFO situation. The group reviewed Swedish sightings back to 1946, individual official reports, press coverage, Soviet press indexes, and spent a day at Wright Field with Air Force investigators. Their briefing to the Intelligence Advisory Committee reported that ATIC carried 20% of 1,500 reports as unexplained, acknowledged 'incredible reports by credible observers,' and identified three danger points: psychological vulnerability, air-defense vulnerability, and the Soviet press silence. The OSE briefing directly led to the January 1953 Robertson Panel.
cases.official: The CIA's own internal review acknowledged a 20% unexplained rate and identified air-defense and psychological vulnerabilities. It recommended further research, coordination with the Research and Development Board, and a public information policy to minimize panic. It explicitly raised psychological warfare implications — both US offensive use and Soviet exploitation of American credulity.
cases.gap: The full OSE Study Group working papers, meeting minutes, and individual member analyses remain missing from the declassified record. The two surviving documents (CIA-RDP81R00560R000100020012-7 and -013-6) are the IAC briefing package, not the underlying study materials. The identity of the specific CIA consultants consulted has not been declassified.
sources: CIA-IAC-1952-012-7 · IAC Flying Saucers briefing package, 36pp (CIA-RDP81R00560R000100020012-7)
CIA-OSE-1952-013-6 · OSE Study Group briefing, 11pp (CIA-RDP81R00560R000100020013-6)

cases.whyItMatters: This is the bureaucratic fingerprint of the CIA entering the UFO field. It documents the chain from the 1952 Washington flap to the 1953 Robertson Panel, and reveals that the CIA's internal assessment was more candid than its public position. The 20% unexplained figure — twice Blue Book's final published 6% — was known internally before the Robertson Panel was convened to manage it.
